metadata.title: Claude, Gated.
metadata.source: Research prompt: How to Connect Claude to LinkedIn (Bear Brown)

skin_warnings: B08: composer beat has an empty spark line — SPARK-LINE LAW wants a short serif cue (e.g. "The change,").

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam, in for Bear. 'Connect Claude to LinkedIn.' Sounds like one task. It's three — and one of the three has no honest answer at all. There's no official LinkedIn connector in Anthropic's directory, so every path means trusting somebody's third-party tool. Let me show you which of the three you can do cleanly, and which one the vendors are quietly lying to you about.

output: Every option = a third-party MCP server Anthropic does NOT security-audit.
The real question splits three ways: Publishing · Analytics · Outreach.

beats.narration_text: Start with what Anthropic actually says. Remote MCP servers are third-party services it does not own, operate, or endorse. It checks directory listings against basic criteria — it does not security-audit any given server. So there is no such thing as 'connecting Claude to LinkedIn' in the abstract. There is only trusting one specific integration, built by one specific stranger, with whatever access you hand it. That's the frame the whole rest of this video hangs on: the question isn't which tool is best. It's which job you're actually doing.

shot.new_visual_element: Directory shows no first-party LinkedIn; third-party trust boundary

beats.narration_text: So here are the three jobs hiding inside one request. One: publishing — posting an announcement to your profile or a company page. Two: analytics — reading follower growth and post impressions on a page you administer. Three: outreach — connection requests, direct messages, prospecting. People say 'connect Claude to LinkedIn' and mean any one of these. They are not the same difficulty. They are not even the same category of legal. And a tool built for one will happily pretend it's safe for another.

Now the single most important fact in this entire subject. Publishing has a real, documented, official LinkedIn API. Analytics has a real, documented, official LinkedIn API. Outreach does not. There is no public way — at any price, at any approval tier — to automate connection requests or cold DMs on an ordinary account. Full stop. Two lanes are open roads. The third is a wall. And that asymmetry is exactly the fact vendor marketing is built to blur, because the third lane is where the money is.

shot.new_visual_element: Two lanes turn green (API), the third hits a wall

beats.narration_text: Here's the real surface, precisely. Personal profile posting: the 'Share on LinkedIn' product, self-serve, instant, fully compliant. Company page posting and analytics: the Community Management API — and that one is not a form, it's an audit. LinkedIn wants a verified company domain, a business email, physical corporate registration, and a screen recording of your actual OAuth flow before it grants production access. Built for agencies, slow for a solo operator, but open. Then the closed doors: generic member-side reading is not accepting new requests. And automated outreach has no scope at all — not gated, not slow, just nonexistent.

shot.new_visual_element: API surface table: self-serve / audit / closed / no-path

beats.act: Act 4 — how the workaround actually works
beats.narration_text: So how do the outreach tools do a thing with no API? They borrow your identity. Many unofficial LinkedIn MCP servers work by extracting your live session cookie — the li_at token, which is effectively your active login — and replaying requests against LinkedIn's internal Voyager API, the one the website uses, not any public endpoint. Some of their setup guides literally tell you to copy that cookie out of your browser. That is handing a stranger your password-equivalent. And LinkedIn runs a layered detector for exactly this: extension signatures, browser fingerprinting, datacenter-IP gating, timing analysis. When a tool markets itself as 'undetectable,' it isn't describing a feature. It's describing what it's evading — and telling you there's something to evade.

shot.new_visual_element: Cookie extraction → Voyager replay vs the detection stack

beats.narration_text: The law here is more careful than either slogan. In hiQ versus LinkedIn, the Ninth Circuit held that scraping data that's public — no login wall — does not violate the Computer Fraud and Abuse Act. That sounds like a green light. It isn't. That ruling does not cover authenticated access behind a login, and it does not touch contract law. LinkedIn's user agreement separately bans bots, scrapers, and automated messaging — and a contract breach lets LinkedIn suspend your account no matter what the federal statute says. hiQ itself proved the point: it settled, paid LinkedIn five hundred thousand dollars, and destroyed the algorithms it built. 'We won't get sued federally' and 'we won't get banned' are two different promises. Vendors quote the first to imply the second.

shot.new_visual_element: CFAA vs contract law split; hiQ settlement stamp

beats.narration_text: Now you can read the vendor pages correctly. They advertise daily send limits, randomized timing to 'simulate human behavior,' and account rotation across several logins. That language is pitched as safety. Flip it over. Those caps and random delays exist to stay under LinkedIn's detection thresholds — not because LinkedIn approved the activity. And multi-account rotation is the loudest tell of all: a tool that needs several identities to hit its advertised volume is admitting, in its own design, that no single account can survive that volume. When the product's safety feature is spreading the risk across more of your accounts, the safety feature is the risk.

shot.new_visual_element: Vendor 'safety' claims flipped to their real meaning

props.sparkLine: The safety feature IS the risk.

beats.narration_text: So here's the lane you actually want, and it's the easy one. Publishing. You register a LinkedIn developer app, attach a company page, and add two self-serve products — Sign In with OpenID Connect, and Share on LinkedIn. No review, no audit. You point Claude at an official-API-only MCP server that authenticates through proper OAuth and never touches a session cookie. Watch the ask.

props.command: claude mcp add linkedin -e LINKEDIN_CLIENT_ID=… -e LINKEDIN_CLIENT_SECRET=… -- node ./linkedin-mcp-server/dist/index.js
output: Scopes: openid · profile · email · w_member_social (all self-serve).
OAuth only — no li_at cookie, no Voyager replay.

beats.narration_text: And here's what Claude is actually doing underneath. Your natural-language 'post this' becomes one call against LinkedIn's documented posts endpoint — a bearer token, your author URN, your text, published to the main feed. A clean two-oh-one, the post's ID handed back in the header. That's the whole trick when the trick is legitimate: no cookie theft, no evasion, no account rotation. One official endpoint, one token you can revoke. Every green thing in this video went through a door like this one.

props.title: POST /rest/posts — the official call
props.code:
curl -X POST 'https://api.linkedin.com/rest/posts' \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $LINKEDIN_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  --data '{
    "author": "urn:li:person:YOUR_PERSON_ID",
    "commentary": "Announcing the new course.",
    "visibility": "PUBLIC",
    "lifecycleState": "PUBLISHED"
  }'

# → 201 Created · post URN in x-restli-id header

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. 'Connect Claude to LinkedIn' is three jobs, not one. Publishing is a self-serve official API — do it, it's a thirty-minute OAuth setup with effectively zero account risk. Company-page analytics is official too, just behind a heavier audit — worth it only if you'll use the data regularly. Outreach automation has no compliant path at any price: the tools that offer it borrow your login and evade a detector, and 'undetectable' is the tell. For real prospecting, the sound default is human-sent outreach with Claude doing the research and the drafting. The efficient-looking tool is the wrong trade — not a shortcut worth taking.

props.artifactHeading: Connect Claude to LinkedIn — the honest map
artifactLines: Publishing: official 'Share on LinkedIn' API, self-serve, ~zero account risk. Just do it.
Company-page analytics: official Community Management API, but a real audit — worth it only if you'll use the data.
Outreach automation: NO compliant path. Cookie-replay tools borrow your login and evade detection; 'undetectable' is the warning label.
Real outreach: human-sent, with Claude researching and drafting. The efficient tool is the wrong trade.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Here's the prompt — read it with me. 'I want to connect Claude to LinkedIn. Before recommending a tool, classify what I actually need, and be explicit about risk instead of defaulting to whichever connector has the slickest onboarding. Here's my goal, my volume, and the account at stake. Tell me plainly whether there's an official-API-backed path — and if the only option is a cookie-replay wrapper or an outreach vendor, say so, explain the real account risk, and flag the red flags: per-day multipliers, multi-account rotation, 'randomized timing.' If my stakes make that tool a bad trade, tell me before giving me setup steps.' That last line is the whole point — it forces Claude to refuse the bad trade instead of just executing it. Paste it, fill in your real numbers, and see which lane you're actually in. Liam, in for Bear.

props.command: I want to connect Claude to LinkedIn. Before recommending a tool, classify what I actually need and be explicit about risk. Goal: [___]. Volume: [___]. Account at stake: [___]. Tell me plainly if there's an official-API path; if the only option is a cookie-replay wrapper or an outreach vendor, say so, explain the real account risk, and flag red flags (per-day multipliers, multi-account rotation, 'randomized timing'). If my stakes make that tool a bad trade, tell me BEFORE giving me setup steps.

beats.narration_text: Claude, Gated. Liam, in for Bear.
